evidence_quotes.quote: planned obsolescence, where companies deliberately push people toward buying newer games instead of respecting the purchases they already made.
Another serious issue is loot boxes and chancebased rewards.
These are designed to keep people spending money for uncertain outcomes, much like gambling.

paragraphs.text: I am writing to share my concern about fairness in videogames, which today make up a huge part of the digital products people use, especially young people. For many, videogames are not just entertainment but a central hobby, community, and even part of their childhood. That is why it feels important to include them in the Digital Fairness Act. A growing problem is that many games people pay for are later shut down completely. When this happens, players lose access to the game itself as well as everything extra they may have bought inside it. This feels deeply unfair, because once a person has paid for something, they should not simply have it taken away. It is hard not to see this as a kind of planned obsolescence, where companies deliberately push people toward buying newer games instead of respecting the purchases they already made. For children and teenagers, this is especially harmful: it is like buying them a toy and then removing it from their room just because the company wants to sell them a different one. Another serious issue is loot boxes and chancebased rewards. These are designed to keep people spending money for uncertain outcomes, much like gambling. Many young players are particularly vulnerable to this kind of design, which can lead them to spend far more than they intended, often without fully understanding the risks. Some countries in Europe already treat loot boxes as gambling, while others do not, which makes the market confusing and unfair. These problems have been known for more than fifteen years, but players have had little protection. An entire generation has grown up with their games and hobbies suddenly disappearing, or with systems that push them to spend more money than they wanted. It should not be normal for people to lose access to something they paid for, or to be exposed to gamblinglike mechanics in their everyday leisure activities. Because videogames are such a large part of the digital world and affect so many people across borders, it makes sense for the EU to pay closer attention to them. I respectfully urge the Commission to consider these issues under the Digital Fairness Act, so that playersespecially minorsare given the same rights and protections as consumers in every other digital market.
